title: Jira & Rally Workflow Models — Vector Recommendations
topic: How do Jira and Rally model workflows / states / status flows for different work item types — and what should Vector's o_flow_system / o_flow_tenant defaulting design look like?
summary: Jira workflow schemes vs Rally ScheduleState/State; Vector should adopt copy-on-create with archetype seed rows.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Overview
  2. 2. Key Findings
  3. 3. Jira Cloud Workflow Model
  4. 4. Rally State Model
  5. 5. Vector — As-Is
  6. 6. Comparison Matrix
  7. 7. Recommendations for Vector
  8. 8. Sources
  9. 9. Gaps

1. Overview

Vector seeds vendor default state-flows in o_flow_system and reads tenant-mutable copies from o_flow_tenant. Migrations 109/110 wrote vendor defaults to o_flow_system only — no copy-on-create hook for new subscriptions, hence the empty "System types" section observed today. Before deciding the shape of the fix, this paper compares Vector's intended model against the two industry references the user named: Jira Cloud and Rally (Broadcom).

The two products diverge sharply. Jira uses a flexible bind-by-reference model (workflow schemes map issue types to global workflows; categorisation is enforced via three fixed Status Categories). Rally uses a locked-list-with-custom-slots model for execution work (ScheduleState) and a per-level fully-configurable model for portfolio items (State). Each gives Vector a different lens on the open question: copy-on-create vs reference, per-level seed, and how to enforce a canonical bucket.

2. Key Findings

  • Jira binds workflows to issue types via a workflow scheme — the scheme is a {issue_type → workflow} map with a default fallback. New custom issue types fall through to the scheme default until explicitly bound. Vector has no scheme analogue today; it copies rows directly into o_flow_tenant.
  • Jira's Status Category (To Do / In Progress / Done) is mandatory on every status, fixed at three values, and used by reporting/JQL. This is structurally close to Vector's canonical_states.clock_role — but Vector splits further (5 roles: none, lead_start, cycle_active, cycle_stop, lead_stop) because it drives time-tracking, not just display.
  • Rally locks the four core ScheduleState values (Defined, In-Progress, Completed, Accepted) workspace-wide and only allows two custom slots — one before, one after. Vector does the opposite: every position is renamable per tenant, and the canonical link is via canonical_code.
  • Rally Portfolio Item State is fully per-level configurable (up to 20 values, ranks, WIP limits, descriptions). Vector's portfolio_item_types row already has its own row in o_flow_tenant per layer — this matches Rally's per-level model.
  • Neither Jira nor Rally has a documented "copy from system_default to tenant on subscription create" hook with the same shape Vector needs. Jira binds-by-reference (no copy); Rally seeds-on-create with a fixed default for new portfolio levels (close to Vector but no per-tenant divergence is captured because there's no tenant boundary in Rally's hierarchy).
  • Vector's design is closer to Jira than Rally — both let admins fully customise state names; both keep a canonical category. The missing piece in Vector is the scheme abstraction (or its functional equivalent: copy-on-provision + ability to reset to vendor default).

3. Jira Cloud Workflow Model

3.1 Workflow definition + scoping

A workflow in Jira is a graph: statuses (named states) as nodes, transitions (directed edges with optional conditions/validators/post-functions) as edges. Workflows come in two scopes: global (company-managed, defined at Jira-admin level, shareable across many projects via a workflow scheme) and project-local (team-managed, each project owns its workflow per work type). Shared workflows cannot be edited in-place while active; edits create a draft of the workflow scheme which is published.

REST surface: GET/POST /rest/api/3/workflows exposes id, name, description, statuses[] (each with id, name, statusCategory) and transitions[] (each with id, name, from[], to, type, optional rules).

3.2 Workflow schemes

A workflow scheme is a named mapping table: { issue_type_id → workflow_id }, plus a default workflow entry that catches all issue types not explicitly mapped. A scheme is associated with one or more company-managed projects; the same scheme can be shared by many projects, so the same issue type uses the same workflow everywhere that scheme is applied — but two projects can bind the same issue type to different workflows by using different schemes. New projects automatically receive the system Default Workflow Scheme (mapping every type to To Do → In Progress → Done); this scheme is system-managed and cannot be configured.

3.3 Status Categories

Jira has exactly three Status Categories, fixed by the system: To Do (key new, grey), In Progress (key indeterminate, blue), Done (key done, green). Every status — built-in or custom — must belong to one of these three. Category is exposed in every status response as statusCategory: { id, key, colorName, name }; JQL exposes it via statusCategory() and statusCategoryChangedDate(). The category is selected at creation and editable later via UI or PUT /rest/api/3/statuses, with broad reporting impact for widely-used statuses.

3.4 Custom issue types and defaulting

When an admin creates a new custom issue type, it is not automatically bound to any specific workflow. Binding happens at the workflow-scheme level: the admin explicitly adds a mapping {new_type → workflow} to each scheme where the type should appear. Without an explicit mapping the type falls through to the scheme's default workflow. In team-managed projects the new work type instead inherits the project's existing simplified workflow (a copy).

3.5 Status creation

Global statuses (company-managed) are created at Jira-admin level (Settings → Issues → Statuses); admin chooses name + category and the status becomes globally available for any workflow. Team-managed projects allow project admins to create statuses scoped to one project. POST /rest/api/3/statuses accepts scope: { type: "GLOBAL" | "PROJECT", project.id }; statusCategory is required.

3.6 Starter workflows / templates

Every new Jira project receives the Jira Default Workflow (To Do → In Progress → Done) via the Default Workflow Scheme. Software projects (Scrum/Kanban) use a Simplified Workflow instead, board-column-driven with no fixed transitions — board admins add columns/statuses without Jira-admin involvement. Scrum simplified default columns: To Do, In Progress, Done. Kanban simplified default: Backlog, Selected for Development, In Progress, Done. Project templates (Scrum, Kanban, Bug Tracking, JSM service desk, etc.) bundle a workflow + issue type scheme + board configuration. There is no public "seed workflow catalogue" endpoint — seeds are baked into project templates rather than exposed as selectable workflow objects.

4. Rally State Model

4.1 ScheduleState (User Stories, Defects, Defect Suites, Risks, Test Sets)

Four locked values workspace-wide: Defined, In-Progress, Completed, Accepted. These cannot be edited, reordered, or disabled. Two optional custom slots exist — one before Defined ("Custom 1") and one after Accepted ("Custom 2") — admins can name these anything. Maximum six values total. The list is workspace-scoped; there is no project-level override. Tasks do not use ScheduleState — they have their own three-value State field (Defined / In-Progress / Completed; no Accepted) which auto-promotes the parent's ScheduleState (all-tasks-Completed → parent goes Completed).

Defects additionally have a separate State field (distinct from ScheduleState) with terminal values Open and Closed locked; middle values (Submitted, Fixed, etc.) are admin-configurable.

4.2 State (Portfolio Items)

Portfolio Items use a separate State field (not ScheduleState). Default seed for every portfolio level: Discovering, Developing, Measuring, Done. State is per-level, not shared — each portfolio item type (Feature, Initiative, Theme, …) has its own independent State list, and admins are explicitly encouraged to define different values per level since lower levels typically need more states. Maximum 20 values per type. Portfolio Kanban columns map 1:1 to State values.

4.3 Portfolio hierarchy

Default three levels: Theme (top) → Initiative → Feature (bottom, attaches User Stories). Workspace admins can add more levels (one source cites "up to five"; TechDocs confirms open configurability without naming a hard cap). Names are fully renameable per workspace; only the lowest level connects to User Stories. The hierarchy is workspace-scoped (one set per workspace, not per project).

4.4 Admin configurability

ScheduleState is the most restrictive — only the two custom slots can be named, and changes propagate workspace-wide across all affected types. Portfolio Item State is the most open — fully configurable per level (name, rank, WIP limit, description, up to 20 values). Rally additionally offers Flow States at the team-board layer: project-scoped many-to-one mappings onto ScheduleState values, used to render the team board. Multiple Flow States can map to a single ScheduleState. There is no documented Jira-style "workflow template" concept where a state list is reusable across types.

4.5 State categories

Rally has no formal status-category system equivalent to Jira's three buckets. Accepted is the implicit "done" sentinel for ScheduleState (Velocity counts only Accepted); for Portfolio Items, Done is simply the last-ranked value with no category annotation. Flow State has an isLastState UI flag for the rightmost board column — UI-only, not a schema-level category.

4.6 Defaulting on type creation

Workspace creation seeds ScheduleState with the four locked values (custom slots empty). New Portfolio Item levels receive the same four defaults (Discovering / Developing / Measuring / Done) — explicitly the same seed for every level. No documented "inherit from parent level" behaviour; whether copy-on-create is a live copy or a reference is not stated in available docs.

4.7 Custom states

ScheduleState — highly restricted (two named custom slots only). Defect State — middle values are admin-extensible. Portfolio Item State — fully open up to 20 values per level. Flow State — fully custom at project scope, but layered on top of ScheduleState rather than replacing it.

5. Vector — As-Is

5.1 Tables

o_flow_system — vendor-seeded default flows per system artefact type. Runtime never reads this table; it is the source-of-truth copy of vendor seeds.

ColumnTypeNotes
idUUID PKgen_random_uuid()
system_artefact_type_idUUIDFK → o_artefact_types_system.id
flow_positionINTCHECK > 0; UNIQUE(type, position)
nameTEXTUNIQUE(type, name)
canonical_codeTEXTFK → canonical_states.code
descriptionTEXTnullable

o_flow_tenant — per-subscription flows. Only flow table the runtime reads. Designed to be copied from o_flow_system on subscription provisioning (hook still TODO; backfill is migration 114), then gadmin/padmin-mutable.

ColumnTypeNotes
idUUID PK
subscription_idUUIDFK → subscriptions.id
system_artefact_type_idUUID nullableFK → o_artefact_types_system.id
tenant_artefact_type_idUUID nullableFK → o_artefact_types_tenant.id
portfolio_item_type_idUUID nullableFK → portfolio_item_types.id
flow_positionINTCHECK > 0; partial uniques per target
nameTEXTpartial uniques per target
canonical_codeTEXTFK → canonical_states.code
archived_atTIMESTAMPTZnullable, soft delete

CHECK o_flow_tenant_target_exactly_one enforces exactly one of the three target FKs is non-null (polymorphic FK pattern).

canonical_states — universal vocabulary, tenant-independent, seeded once. code PK; clock_role ENUM CHECK over 'none' | 'lead_start' | 'cycle_active' | 'cycle_stop' | 'lead_stop'. Current seeds:

codelabelclock_rolesort
backlogBacklognone10
readyReadylead_start20
doingDoingcycle_active30
completedCompletedcycle_stop40
acceptedAcceptedlead_stop50

5.2 Vendor seeds (o_flow_system)

Six system types, all with a 5-state flow except tasks (3-state, no backlog/accepted):

TypeFlow
execution_work_itemsBacklog → Ready → Doing → Completed → Accepted
execution_defectsBacklog → Ready → Doing → Completed → Accepted
execution_tasksReady → Doing → Completed
execution_test_casesBacklog → Ready → Doing → Completed → Accepted
execution_epicsBacklog → Ready → Doing → Completed → Accepted
strategicBacklog → Ready → Doing → Completed → Accepted

5.3 Backend

flows.Service exposes three list methods (listSystem / listTenant / listPortfolio). All three query o_flow_tenant exclusively, scoped by subscription_id, joined to the appropriate target table for labels. FlowsManage permission code flows.manage is granted to gadmin (rank 30) and padmin (rank 25).

5.4 What migration 114 does

One-shot backfill: INSERT INTO o_flow_tenant ... SELECT FROM subscriptions CROSS JOIN o_flow_system ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING. Idempotent. Covers system types only. Does not wire a subscription-create hook — new subscriptions after this migration still have empty flows unless explicitly seeded.

6. Comparison Matrix

ConceptJira CloudRallyVector (as-is)Vector (proposed)
Vendor default lives in System "Default Workflow" baked into Default Workflow Scheme Hardcoded ScheduleState (4 values) + per-PI-type seed (4 values) o_flow_system (28 rows: 6 types × 5/3 positions) Keep o_flow_system; add archetype row for portfolio default
Bind type → flow Workflow Scheme ({type → workflow_id}, default fallback) Implicit (ScheduleState is workspace-wide; PI State is per-type) Direct rows in o_flow_tenant with polymorphic target FK Keep direct rows; add copy-on-create hook on subscription + tenant-type create
Custom type defaulting Falls through to scheme default workflow (or explicit bind) PI: seed with Discovering/Developing/Measuring/Done; tenant types n/a None — empty list (manual add) Tenant type: copy from archetype; portfolio_item_types: copy from portfolio_archetype
Canonical category Status Category (To Do / In Progress / Done) — required, 3 fixed buckets None formally — Accepted is implicit done sentinel canonical_states.clock_role — 5 buckets, drives time-tracking Keep clock_role; expose category UI for admin clarity
Custom state in middle of flow Yes (any number, must pick category) ScheduleState: no. PI State: yes (up to 20) Yes (per o_flow_tenant position) — must pick canonical_code Same — already aligned
Reset to default Workflow scheme "copy default" (admin action) Per-PI-type only; ScheduleState locked None — would require admin to delete all rows + re-seed Add "Reset to vendor default" per type (re-copy from o_flow_system)
Copy semantics Reference (project ↔ scheme ↔ workflow) Copy-on-create (each PI level has own list) Copy-on-create (intended; backfilled today by mig 114) Copy-on-create — same
Per-tenant divergence Per-project (via different schemes) Workspace-wide for ScheduleState; per-PI-type for PI State Per-subscription (every row carries subscription_id) Same — already aligned

7. Recommendations for Vector

7.1 Adopt copy-on-create over scheme-by-reference

Vector is closer to Rally than Jira on this axis: every flow row already lives under a specific subscription_id, and the polymorphic target FK lets a tenant edit any row without affecting any other tenant. Switching to a Jira-style scheme-by-reference would require an indirection layer (flow_scheme + flow_scheme_binding) that doesn't pay for itself given Vector's single-flow-per-type-per-tenant model. Stay copy-on-create.

7.2 Keep canonical_states as the bucket — but expose it as "category" in UI

Vector's clock_role is structurally Jira's Status Category, just with five buckets instead of three because it drives lead/cycle time-tracking. Keep the five roles; the Work Items tab UI should add a small "Category" column rendering the bucket as a chip (Backlog / Active / Done rolled up from the 5 roles) so admins reading the table can see at a glance what each state means without needing to know the canonical-code vocabulary.

7.3 Add a portfolio-archetype seed (single row in o_flow_system)

Today o_flow_system has six rows, one per system artefact type. Portfolio item types have no vendor default in o_flow_system — migration 110 separately did CROSS JOIN portfolio_item_types × canonical_states, which is fine for existing layers but doesn't help when a tenant creates a new layer (Theme, Programme, etc.). Mirror Rally's approach: add one archetype row in o_flow_system with a sentinel system_artefact_type_id (or a new dedicated portfolio_archetype column / row marker), and have the "create portfolio layer" flow seed o_flow_tenant from that archetype.

7.4 Add a tenant-type-create hook with the same archetype pattern

When a tenant creates a custom artefact type (o_artefact_types_tenant), the same archetype should be copied. This is the equivalent of Jira's "fall through to scheme default" — except realised by copying rows rather than referencing a workflow_id. The user gets a workable starting state instead of an empty list.

7.5 Ship a "Reset to system default" action

Both Jira (workflow scheme reset) and Rally (per-PI-type re-seed) implicitly support this; Vector should too. Specifically: a button on each row of the Work Items tab → "Reset to vendor default" → backend transactionally archives current rows for that subscription+target and re-copies from o_flow_system. This is the escape hatch when an admin breaks their flow.

7.6 Wire the subscription-create hook (the long-overdue piece)

Migration 114 backfills existing subscriptions only. The actual bug is that subscription provisioning never copied vendor defaults. This wants a Go-side hook in the subscription-create path: after INSERT INTO subscriptions, do the same INSERT INTO o_flow_tenant ... SELECT FROM o_flow_system WHERE subscription_id = $new. Inside a single transaction so a partial subscription never exists. Plus per-portfolio-layer seed if 7.3 is adopted.

7.7 Schema additions for next migration (115+)

#MigrationEffect
115Add portfolio archetype row to o_flow_systemSingle sentinel row for portfolio_item_types defaulting
116Add tenant-archetype row to o_flow_systemDefault 5-state flow for new o_artefact_types_tenant rows
117Backend: subscription-create hookCopy o_flow_system → o_flow_tenant in transaction with subscription INSERT
118Backend: tenant-type-create / portfolio-layer-create hooksCopy archetype to o_flow_tenant at type-creation time
119Backend: flows.Reset(target) handlerArchive current + re-copy from o_flow_system; gated on flows.manage

7.8 Migration 114 — proceed as written

The pending backfill is the right shape for the existing-data problem. Recommendations 7.3–7.7 are forward-looking and don't change what migration 114 needs to do. Apply 114 as drafted. The filename collision with the uncommitted 114_remove_portfolio_model_from_sidebar.sql needs to be resolved first — renumber whichever was added second.

9. Gaps

  • Jira v3 statusCategory key strings not directly verified from v3 docs (v2 confirms new / indeterminate / done; v3 likely identical but not explicitly cited).
  • Whether a Jira admin can change a status's category from the UI (vs API only) was not explicitly confirmed.
  • POST /rest/api/3/workflows full request schema not retrieved — would need an OpenAPI fetch.
  • Rally portfolio hierarchy max depth — one source cites "up to five"; TechDocs confirms open configurability without naming a hard cap. Vector cap: 10 levels (confirmed by user, 2026-05-05).
  • Rally state inheritance mechanics when a new portfolio level is created — copy-on-create vs shared-by-reference vs fresh seed — not stated in available docs.
  • Rally Task State configurability — whether the three-value Task State is locked or admin-editable not confirmed in available TechDocs.
  • Rally REST WSAPI ScheduleState AllowedValues not cross-checked against the UI docs (auth required).
  • Vector portfolio archetype design — the recommendation to add a single sentinel row in o_flow_system may collide with the existing CHECK constraint that system_artefact_type_id is NOT NULL; needs schema-design pass before the next migration is written.
